design_philosophy.core_loop: Explore dungeon-style ruins inspired by real-world locations, defeat room-clearing enemy encounters to gain permanent stat jewels, acquire character-gated abilities to access new areas, progress toward collecting Mystic Statues
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Navigation puzzle (area-gating via character abilities) vs. combat encounter management; resource scarcity (healing herbs) forces careful progression
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Global explorer unraveling ancient mysteries across real-world ruins while gaining transformative powers; protagonist discovering hidden identity as Dark Knight
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately removes modern RPG conveniences: no experience points, no currency, no equipment, no revisiting areas (except late game), single healing item type, fixed difficulty. Story progression gates access more than player choice. Emphasizes permanent character transformation (Will → Freedan → Shadow) as core mechanic, not cosmetic. No quest markers or side content except Red Jewel collection (which can become uncompletable). Knowledge-gated exploration over mechanical grinding.

difficulty.lives_system: Will loses all lives on health depletion; reaching 100+ accumulated life points from defeated enemies grants resurrection at stage start instead of game over
difficulty.checkpointing: Dark Spaces function as save points and safe rest zones; scattered throughout combat and non-combat areas. Allows health recovery and occasional form-switching or ability acquisition
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Fixed difficulty setting with no selectable modes. Player agency limited to resource conservation (herb use, life point farming) and optional Red Jewel collection

progression.upgrade_system: Jewel-based stat progression: defeating all enemies in a room grants permanent increases to attack, defense, or health (one-time bonus per room even if enemies respawn). Character transformation tree: Will acquires Freedan form, later gains Shadow form; each form has unique combat and traversal abilities. Mystic Statue artifact collection (story-gated)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Story progression and character form acquisition unlock new areas. Freedan's combat-oriented abilities (longer reach, higher damage) and Shadow's high damage output gate content. Will's flute abilities focus on environmental access (puzzle-solving over combat)
replayability_hooks: Red Jewel collection sidequest (completable only on first playthrough; missable items create permanent consequences)
Multiple playable characters with distinct ability trees and playstyles (Will vs. Freedan vs. Shadow)
Non-revisitable areas create single-playthrough finality and discovery pressure
Ambiguous ending leaves interpretation open regarding memory loss and real-world connections

world_building_techniques: Real-world location theming: Great Pyramid, Great Wall of China, Angkor Wat, Nazca Lines, Incan ruins as dungeon settings with fantasy overlay
Historical framing: Age of Exploration (16th century) with explorer protagonist context
Environmental storytelling: Each ruin holds puzzle pieces revealing Tower of Babel's secrets and comet's true nature
NPC dialogue and character discovery: Will's identity as Dark Knight revealed through story progression rather than exposition
Magical world-state changes: Comet approach as visible apocalyptic threat; Dark Gaia manifestation as final confrontation
Memory ambiguity: Final ending suggests real-world anchoring while erasing in-game memories, blurring fantasy-reality boundary

boss_design.philosophy: Bosses serve as climactic dungeon guardians and story pillars rather than grinding encounters. Non-revisitable (defeat once per playthrough) reinforces exploration finality. Pattern-recognizable but damage-tier varies by player character form
notable_bosses: Dark Gaia (final form of comet; climactic story confrontation; requires Will and Kara to unite as Shadow)
Dungeon bosses at real-world ruins (Angkor Wat, Pyramid, etc.; story-integrated encounters)

faction_indicators: Will (Light Knight) vs. Freedan (Dark Knight) mechanical and thematic dichotomy
Kara (Light Knight counterpart) as narrative equal and co-savior
Gaia as cryptic world-spirit guide with ambiguous motives (tentacled, otherworldly appearance)
danger_signals: Comet approach as civilization-ending threat (reshapes world evolution)
Ancient Blazer War remnants (comet as weaponized relic)
Memory erasure as cost of salvation (both heroes forget adventure)
Non-linear time/reality: final scene ambiguously set in 'modern day school' despite 16th-century setting
environmental_storytelling: Ruined civilizations at each location hint at cyclical destruction from comet returns
Mystic Statues as artifact collection mechanic embedding lore acquisition into progression
Dark Spaces as liminal zones where Gaia appears; bridges between story beats and mechanical checkpoints
Tower of Babel as convergence point for all collected knowledge and final revelation space
